title: Insertion and deletion

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Insertion and deletion; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Algorithmic Foundations; August 2023; material applicable to Insertion and deletion
sources.claim: Linked-list insertion and deletion rewire a bounded number of links once the target node or predecessor is known.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Linked-list insertion and deletion rewire a bounded number of links once the target node or predecessor is known.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Deleting after prev stores prev.next, links around it, then releases or abandons the removed node safely. Your goal is to implement, debug and test list insertion and deletion across empty, singleton and multi-node cases. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Array representation and operations; Singly linked representation; Correctness and counterexamples. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: Linked-list insertion and deletion rewire a bounded number of links once the target node or predecessor is known. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: In C, nodes need explicit allocation, links, and cleanup; in C++, prefer RAII ownership such as unique_ptr where it fits; in Java, node references are garbage-collected but links and equality remain explicit; in Python, object references simplify storage but do not change linear traversal complexity.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Finding that position can still be linear, and head, tail, empty, and singleton cases need separate invariants. The draft therefore rejects: Deleting an item by value is always O(1) in a linked list.

includes: Update links correctly at head, middle and tail while preserving ownership and size invariants.
Implement, debug and test list insertion and deletion across empty, singleton and multi-node cases.

In C, nodes need explicit allocation, links, and cleanup; in C++, prefer RAII ownership such as unique_ptr where it fits; in Java, node references are garbage-collected but links and equality remain explicit; in Python, object references simplify storage but do not change linear traversal complexity.
excludes: Finding that position can still be linear, and head, tail, empty, and singleton cases need separate invariants.

outcomes: Implement, debug and test list insertion and deletion across empty, singleton and multi-node cases.

examples.title: Insertion and deletion: language-neutral trace
examples.situation: Deleting after prev stores prev.next, links around it, then releases or abandons the removed node safely.
walkthrough: Step 1 — State the abstract input and invariant: Deleting after prev stores prev.next, links around it, then releases or abandons the removed node safely.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Link removal is O(1) after locating the predecessor, but locating a value is generally O(n).
examples.result: The conceptual result follows because Link removal is O(1) after locating the predecessor, but locating a value is generally O(n).

examples.title: Insertion and deletion: implementation transfer
examples.situation: In C, nodes need explicit allocation, links, and cleanup; in C++, prefer RAII ownership such as unique_ptr where it fits; in Java, node references are garbage-collected but links and equality remain explicit; in Python, object references simplify storage but do not change linear traversal complexity.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Keep the same specification and invariant across all four languages.

Step 2 — Map storage, library, equality, bounds, ownership, and error rules using: In C, nodes need explicit allocation, links, and cleanup; in C++, prefer RAII ownership such as unique_ptr where it fits; in Java, node references are garbage-collected but links and equality remain explicit; in Python, object references simplify storage but do not change linear traversal complexity.
Step 3 — Retest this boundary in the selected implementation: Finding that position can still be linear, and head, tail, empty, and singleton cases need separate invariants.
examples.result: Concept mastery transfers, while implementation evidence remains language-specific.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Deleting an item by value is always O(1) in a linked list.
misconceptions.correction: Link removal is O(1) after locating the predecessor, but locating a value is generally O(n).
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use an invariant or counterexample to explain why this DSA belief fails: Deleting an item by value is always O(1) in a linked list.

usefulFor: Introducing structure, order, connectivity, or state transition before formal notation
limitations: A physical arrangement does not reproduce asymptotic cost, aliasing, numeric limits, recursion depth, or the four languages' implementation rules.
